AL.commercialThreshold: All commercial construction requires permit; threshold varies by locality
AL.exemptions: Minor repairs, painting, flooring, cabinet work (no structural, electrical, plumbing)
AL.notes: Alabama adopted statewide building codes in 2012. Some rural areas lack local enforcement.
AL.permitFeeStructure: Varies by jurisdiction; typically $X per $1,000 of construction value or per sq ft
AL.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction (city or county building department)
AL.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; repairs/alterations above $2,500 often require permit
AL.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Alabama Building Commission adopts IBC; effective statewide except where stricter local codes apply

AK.commercialThreshold: All commercial construction requires permit where local jurisdiction exists; state code in unorganized areas
AK.exemptions: Agricultural structures in rural areas; minor maintenance
AK.notes: Alaska does not have a uniform statewide building code authority outside fire safety. Permafrost and seismic requirements are significant local concerns.
AK.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction or State of Alaska (unorganized areas)
AK.residentialThreshold: Varies by jurisdiction; permit typically required for all new construction
AK.stateBuildingOffice: Alaska Division of Fire and Life Safety (fire code); local building departments for building code
AK.statewideBuildingCode: Partial - State Fire Marshal enforces fire code; no single mandatory statewide building code; Anchorage and Fairbanks have own codes

AZ.exemptions: Minor repairs, painting, decorative work; agricultural structures
AZ.notes: Maricopa County and Pima County have county-level building departments. Some rural areas have limited enforcement capacity.
AZ.permitFeeStructure: Varies by jurisdiction; typically plan check fee plus permit fee based on valuation
AZ.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; repairs/alterations above $1,000 typically require permit
AZ.stateBuildingOffice: Arizona Department of Fire, Building and Life Safety (manufactured housing and state buildings)
AZ.statewideBuildingCode: Partial - Arizona adopts model codes for manufactured housing; cities/counties adopt local codes typically based on IBC

AR.commercialThreshold: All commercial construction requires permit in incorporated areas
AR.exemptions: Farm buildings; structures in unincorporated areas without local ordinance; minor repairs
AR.notes: Many rural counties in Arkansas have no building permit requirement. Contractors should verify local requirements.
AR.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction; State Fire Prevention Code administered by State Police
AR.residentialThreshold: Permit required for all new residential construction; many rural jurisdictions have no permit requirement
AR.stateBuildingOffice: Arkansas State Police - Fire Marshal Division
AR.statewideBuildingCode: Partial - Arkansas Fire Prevention Code is statewide; local building codes vary by jurisdiction

CA.citation: Cal. Health & Safety Code § 18900 et seq.; Title 24 CCR
CA.exemptions: Painting, papering, tiling ≤$500 value; one-story detached accessory structures ≤120 sq ft in some jurisdictions; fences ≤6 ft
CA.notes: California Title 24 is one of the most comprehensive building code systems in the nation. CALGreen (California Green Building Standards Code) adds additional sustainability requirements. High fire hazard severity zone requirements apply in many areas.
CA.permitFeeStructure: Varies significantly by jurisdiction; plan check fees plus permit fees based on project valuation; some cities charge $10-20+ per sq ft
CA.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction (city or county building department); DSA for state-funded K-12/community colleges; OSHPD for healthcare
CA.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alteration/addition above $500 typically requires permit
CA.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - California Building Standards Code (Title 24) mandatory statewide

CO.citation: C.R.S. § 24-33-502 et seq. (fire code); local municipal codes
CO.commercialThreshold: All commercial construction requires permit in municipalities
CO.exemptions: Unincorporated rural areas may have no permit requirements; minor maintenance and repairs
CO.notes: Colorado has no statewide building code. Counties and municipalities adopt their own. Wildfire construction requirements are increasingly common in many areas.
CO.residentialThreshold: Varies by jurisdiction; permit required for all new construction in most municipalities
CO.stateBuildingOffice: No single state building office; local jurisdictions adopt and enforce codes
CO.statewideBuildingCode: No - No mandatory statewide building code; municipalities adopt own codes

CT.exemptions: Minor repairs, painting, decorating under $10,000 with no structural/MEP changes
CT.notes: Connecticut updates its State Building Code periodically. All municipalities must adopt and enforce the state code. Towns have building officials who issue permits.
CT.permitFeeStructure: Set by each municipality; typically $X per $1,000 valuation; state surcharge applies
CT.residentialThreshold: Permit required for all new residential construction; renovations/alterations above $10,000 or involving structural work require permit
CT.stateBuildingOffice: Connecticut Department of Administrative Services - State Building Inspector
CT.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Connecticut State Building Code mandatory statewide (2022 update based on IBC 2021)

DE.citation: 16 Del. Code § 7101 et seq. (State Fire Prevention Regulations and Building Code)
DE.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; minor repairs under $500; maintenance items
DE.notes: Delaware is unique in having the State Fire Marshal administer building codes. New Castle, Kent, and Sussex counties all have local enforcement mechanisms as well.
DE.permitFeeStructure: Based on construction value; $10 per $1,000 valuation typical; county-specific
DE.permittingAuthority: Office of State Fire Marshal (statewide); local jurisdiction for many permits
DE.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; additions/alterations above $5,000 typically require permit
DE.stateBuildingOffice: Delaware Office of State Fire Marshal - Office of Building Code Compliance
DE.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Delaware Building Code adopted by State Fire Marshal; based on IBC

DC.citation: District of Columbia Construction Codes and DOB permitting regulations
DC.exemptions: Minor cosmetic work not affecting structure, life safety, or MEP systems
DC.notes: District permitting is centralized through DOB e-permitting workflows with discipline-specific review and inspections.
DC.permitFeeStructure: District fee schedules are based on project valuation, permit category, and trade scope
DC.permittingAuthority: District of Columbia Department of Buildings (DOB)
DC.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; structural, electrical, plumbing, and mechanical alterations require permit
DC.stateBuildingOffice: District of Columbia Department of Buildings (DOB)
DC.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - District Construction Codes (with local amendments) apply district-wide

adoptedCodes: FBC 7th Edition 2020 (based on IBC/IRC with FL amendments)
FL.citation: Fla. Stat. § 553.70 et seq. (Florida Building Act)
FL.exemptions: Minor repairs under $2,500 that do not affect structural, electrical, plumbing, or fire protection systems
FL.notes: Florida Building Code includes special hurricane/wind load requirements. High-velocity hurricane zones (Miami-Dade and Broward) have stricter wind resistance requirements. Product approval required for many components.
FL.permitFeeStructure: Varies by county; typically 1-3% of project cost or per sq ft; many counties charge $7-15/sq ft for new construction
FL.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction (county or city building department)
FL.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; additions/renovations above $2,500 typically require permit; re-roofing always requires permit
FL.stateBuildingOffice: Florida Department of Business and Professional Regulation (DBPR) - Building Codes Program
FL.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Florida Building Code (FBC) mandatory statewide; updated every 3 years

GA.exemptions: Minor repairs; agricultural buildings in unincorporated areas; detached accessory structures ≤120 sq ft
GA.notes: Georgia adopts state minimum codes; local governments may adopt more stringent codes. Coastal construction zone has additional requirements.
GA.permitFeeStructure: Varies by county; typically based on construction value
GA.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction (county or city building department); DCA for technical codes
GA.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; renovations involving structural, electrical, plumbing, or mechanical require permit
GA.stateBuildingOffice: Georgia Department of Community Affairs (DCA) - Construction Codes and Housing
GA.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Georgia State Minimum Standard Codes adopted by Department of Community Affairs (DCA)

HI.exemptions: Minor repairs under $1,000; maintenance items; temporary structures
HI.notes: Hawaii has no statewide building code; each of the four counties (Honolulu, Hawaii, Maui, Kauai) has its own codes. Solar and hurricane construction requirements important throughout.
HI.permitFeeStructure: Varies by county; Honolulu: $45-75+ per $1,000 valuation
HI.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations above $1,000 typically require permit
HI.stateBuildingOffice: Hawaii Department of Accounting and General Services (DAGS) for state facilities; county building departments for private construction
HI.statewideBuildingCode: Partial - Each county adopts its own building code, typically based on IBC

ID.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; structures in unincorporated areas without local ordinance
ID.notes: Idaho Division of Building Safety has jurisdiction over manufactured homes, elevators, electrical, plumbing, and HVAC statewide, plus building code in areas without local codes.
ID.permitFeeStructure: Varies by jurisdiction; DBS: based on construction value
ID.residentialThreshold: Varies by jurisdiction; permit required for all new construction in most areas
ID.statewideBuildingCode: Partial - Division of Building Safety (DBS) has statewide authority for specific occupancy types; local codes for most residential/commercial

adoptedCodes: IBC 2021 (Chicago: Chicago Building Code unique to city)
IL.exemptions: Some rural unincorporated areas have no permit requirement; agricultural buildings
IL.notes: Chicago has its own unique building code (not based on IBC). Rest of Illinois relies on local adoption of model codes. No statewide residential building code.
IL.permitFeeStructure: Varies significantly; Chicago has complex fee structure; suburbs vary widely
IL.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit in municipalities
IL.stateBuildingOffice: Illinois Capital Development Board (CDB) for state facilities
IL.statewideBuildingCode: Partial - No single mandatory statewide code; Capital Development Board (CDB) adopts codes for state facilities; municipalities adopt own codes

IN.commercialThreshold: All commercial/non-residential construction requires permit from IDHS or local jurisdiction
IN.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; private detached residential accessory structures under 200 sq ft
IN.notes: Indiana has a dual system: IDHS enforces state commercial building code; local jurisdictions enforce residential codes. Indiana has preempted local residential code adoption in some respects.
IN.permitFeeStructure: IDHS commercial: $0.07-0.12 per sq ft; local residential varies
IN.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction for residential; IDHS for commercial/non-residential
IN.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit from local building department
IN.stateBuildingOffice: Indiana Department of Homeland Security (IDHS) - Fire and Building Safety Division
IN.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Indiana has statewide building codes enforced by Indiana Department of Homeland Security (IDHS) for commercial; local for residential

IA.citation: Iowa Code § 103A (State Building Code Act); § 100B (Fire Safety)
IA.commercialThreshold: All commercial construction requires permit in municipalities
IA.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; rural unincorporated areas without local codes; minor repairs
IA.notes: Iowa Building Code Act allows but does not mandate local adoption. Most urban areas have adopted codes. Rural areas may have limited requirements.
IA.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction; Iowa State Fire Marshal for state buildings and fire code
IA.residentialThreshold: Permit required in most municipalities; no statewide residential permit requirement
IA.statewideBuildingCode: Partial - Iowa State Fire Code (IFC) mandatory statewide; building codes adopted by localities

KS.citation: K.S.A. 31-133 et seq. (State Fire Marshal Act); local municipal codes
KS.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; structures in unincorporated areas without local ordinance; minor repairs
KS.notes: Kansas has no statewide building code mandate for local governments. Local adoption varies widely. Tornado/wind construction requirements important.
KS.residentialThreshold: Varies by jurisdiction; permit required in most cities
KS.statewideBuildingCode: Partial - No mandatory statewide residential building code; State Fire Marshal code for most buildings

adoptedCodes: KBC (based on IBC 2018 with KY amendments)
KY.commercialThreshold: All commercial construction requires state permit
KY.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; detached accessory structures under 200 sq ft
KY.notes: Kentucky has a statewide building code system. DHBC handles commercial permits; local building departments handle residential in most areas.
KY.permitFeeStructure: DHBC: $0.05-0.10 per sq ft for commercial; residential varies by locality
KY.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction; Department of Housing, Buildings and Construction (DHBC) for commercial/non-residential
KY.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; additions/renovations involving structural or MEP require permit
KY.stateBuildingOffice: Kentucky Department of Housing, Buildings and Construction (DHBC)
KY.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Kentucky Building Code mandatory statewide for commercial; Kentucky Residential Code for new residential

LA.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; minor repairs under $5,000; structures under 400 sq ft on residential lots
LA.notes: Louisiana adopted the LSUCC in 2005 after Hurricane Katrina to standardize building requirements. Wind and flood construction requirements are significant along the Gulf Coast.
LA.permitFeeStructure: Varies by parish/municipality; typically based on valuation
LA.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction (parish or municipal building department); State Fire Marshal for certain occupancies
LA.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations/additions involving structural or MEP require permit
LA.stateBuildingOffice: Louisiana State Fire Marshal - Office of the State Fire Marshal
LA.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Louisiana State Uniform Construction Code (LSUCC) mandatory statewide based on IBC

adoptedCodes: IRC 2015 (Maine Uniform Building and Energy Code for 1-2 family)
ME.citation: 30-A M.R.S.A. § 4101 et seq. (Uniform Building and Energy Code)
ME.commercialThreshold: Commercial permits required by local jurisdiction
ME.exemptions: Buildings in municipalities with <2,000 population or <10 permits/year may be exempt from state code; farm buildings
ME.notes: Maine adopted the Uniform Building and Energy Code (UBEC) for 1-2 family dwellings. Small municipalities (population <4,000) may opt out.
ME.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction; Maine Building Codes and Standards (DACF) for state buildings
ME.residentialThreshold: State requires permits for new 1-2 family dwellings; local jurisdictions set other thresholds
ME.stateBuildingOffice: Maine Department of Agriculture, Conservation and Forestry (DACF) - Building Codes
ME.statewideBuildingCode: Partial - Mandatory for 1-2 family dwellings and manufactured housing under state code; rest by local adoption

MD.citation: Md. Code Ann., Bus. Reg. § 8-101 et seq.; COMAR 09.12.50 et seq.
MD.exemptions: Agricultural buildings (partial); minor repairs under $5,000 not affecting structural/MEP
MD.notes: Maryland Building Performance Standards apply statewide but counties enforce locally with some variations. MDE approvals also required for many projects.
MD.permitFeeStructure: Varies by county; typically based on construction value; Montgomery County among highest fees
MD.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations involving structural, electrical, plumbing, or mechanical require permit
MD.stateBuildingOffice: Maryland Department of Labor - Division of Labor and Industry (Code Administration)
MD.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Maryland Building Performance Standards (MBPS) mandatory statewide

adoptedCodes: 780 CMR (based on IBC 2021 with MA amendments)
IECC 2021 (Stretch Energy Code in many communities)
MA.exemptions: Minor repairs not involving structural/MEP under $1,000; maintenance items; one-story accessory structures ≤120 sq ft
MA.notes: Massachusetts has a strong statewide building code. Many communities have adopted the Stretch Energy Code with more stringent energy requirements. Seismic requirements apply. BBRS reviews and updates code.
MA.permitFeeStructure: Set by each municipality; BBRS sets minimum standards; typical $X per $1,000 valuation
MA.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction (building inspector or building commissioner) under state delegation
MA.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations/additions involving structural or MEP require permit
MA.stateBuildingOffice: Massachusetts Board of Building Regulations and Standards (BBRS) - BBRS
MA.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Massachusetts State Building Code (780 CMR) mandatory statewide

adoptedCodes: Michigan Energy Code (based on IECC 2015 with amendments)
MI.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; minor repairs under $10,000 not affecting structural integrity or MEP systems
MI.notes: Michigan Bureau of Construction Codes provides oversight of the statewide building code program. Local governments are agents of the state for code enforcement.
MI.permitFeeStructure: Varies by local unit; state-authorized fee schedule; typically based on construction value
MI.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction (city, township, or county); LARA for state building code oversight
MI.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations/additions above $10,000 or involving structural/MEP require permit
MI.stateBuildingOffice: Michigan Department of Licensing and Regulatory Affairs (LARA) - Bureau of Construction Codes (BCC)
MI.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Michigan Building Code (MBC) mandatory statewide based on IBC

adoptedCodes: Minnesota Energy Code (IECC 2021 with MN amendments)
MN.citation: Minn. Stat. § 326B.02 et seq. (Minnesota State Building Code)
MN.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; farm homes (partial); garages and accessory structures under 200 sq ft with no utilities
MN.notes: Minnesota has a strong statewide code program. State Building Official oversees local enforcement. Snow load and cold climate construction requirements are significant.
MN.permitFeeStructure: Set by each municipality; state has minimum fee schedule; typical based on valuation
MN.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction (city or county) under state delegation
MN.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations/additions requiring structural changes or MEP permit
MN.stateBuildingOffice: Minnesota Department of Labor and Industry (DLI) - Construction Codes and Licensing
MN.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Minnesota State Building Code (MSBC) mandatory statewide

MS.citation: Miss. Code Ann. § 75-76-1 et seq.; local municipal codes
MS.commercialThreshold: Commercial permits required in incorporated areas with codes
MS.exemptions: Many unincorporated rural areas have no permit system
MS.notes: Mississippi does not have a statewide building code mandate for local governments. Code adoption and enforcement varies widely by locality. Coastal areas have enhanced wind and flood requirements.
MS.permitFeeStructure: Varies by jurisdiction; many rural areas have no fee structure
MS.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction (city or county); no mandatory statewide code for private buildings
MS.residentialThreshold: Varies by jurisdiction; many rural areas have no permit requirement
MS.stateBuildingOffice: Mississippi State Fire Marshal Office (fire code); no statewide building code office
MS.statewideBuildingCode: Partial - State Board of Health enforces code for certain facilities; IBC adopted by many municipalities voluntarily

MO.citation: Mo. Rev. Stat. § 320.010 et seq. (Fire Marshal); local municipal codes
MO.commercialThreshold: All commercial construction requires permit in municipalities
MO.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; unincorporated areas without local codes; minor repairs
MO.notes: Missouri has no statewide building code mandate. Code adoption by localities varies. St. Louis and Kansas City metro areas have comprehensive permit programs.
MO.residentialThreshold: Permit required in most municipalities; rural areas may have no requirement
MO.statewideBuildingCode: Partial - State Fire Marshal enforces fire code; no statewide building code mandate for local governments

MT.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; single-family homes on agricultural land (partial); farm buildings
MT.notes: Montana has a statewide building code program. In areas without local enforcement, Montana Building Codes Bureau provides permit and inspection services.
MT.permitFeeStructure: Montana Building Codes Bureau: fee schedule based on construction value; local jurisdictions vary
MT.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction; Montana Building Codes Bureau for commercial permits in many areas
MT.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; many rural areas rely on state permit office
MT.stateBuildingOffice: Montana Department of Labor and Industry (DLI) - Building Codes Bureau
MT.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Montana Building Codes Act provides statewide code; administered by MBCC

NE.citation: Neb. Rev. Stat. § 81-502 et seq. (State Fire Marshal); local codes
NE.commercialThreshold: All commercial construction requires permit in incorporated areas
NE.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; unincorporated areas; minor maintenance
NE.notes: Nebraska does not have a statewide building code mandate for local governments. Major metro areas (Omaha, Lincoln) have comprehensive permit programs.
NE.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction (city or county); State Fire Marshal for fire code
NE.residentialThreshold: Varies by jurisdiction; permit required in most cities and villages
NE.statewideBuildingCode: Partial - State Fire Marshal enforces fire code; no mandatory statewide building code for local governments

NV.citation: NRS § 278.580 et seq. (building codes); NRS § 444.430 (manufactured housing)
NV.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; detached accessory structures under 200 sq ft (varies by jurisdiction)
NV.notes: Nevada requires local governments to adopt the model codes. Seismic and high wind requirements apply in many areas. Clark County (Las Vegas) processes very high permit volumes.
NV.permitFeeStructure: Varies by county; Clark County (Las Vegas): significant fee schedule; Washoe County (Reno): moderate fees
NV.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations/additions above $1,500 typically require permit
NV.stateBuildingOffice: Nevada State Public Works Division for state facilities; local jurisdictions for private construction
NV.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Nevada Revised Statutes require adoption of model codes by local governments; State Public Works Division for state buildings

NH.citation: RSA 155-A (New Hampshire Building Code); RSA 674 (Local Land Use)
NH.exemptions: Minor repairs under $5,000; agricultural buildings; maintenance items
NH.notes: New Hampshire adopted the NH Building Code for statewide commercial construction. Local zoning also applies.
NH.permitFeeStructure: Varies by municipality; typically based on construction value
NH.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction (city or town building inspector)
NH.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations/additions involving structural or MEP require permit
NH.stateBuildingOffice: New Hampshire Office of Professional Licensure and Certification (OPLC) - State Building Code Review Board
NH.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - New Hampshire Building Code (based on IBC) required for commercial; residential code for 1-2 family dwellings

adoptedCodes: NJUCC (based on IBC 2021 with NJ amendments)
NJ.exemptions: Minor repairs under $500; maintenance items; painting and decorating; detached accessory structures ≤200 sq ft with no utilities (some jurisdictions)
NJ.notes: New Jersey UCC is administered by DCA with local enforcement. Very comprehensive system with multiple subcodes (building, fire, electrical, plumbing, energy). State DOE energy code applies.
NJ.permitFeeStructure: NJ DCA establishes fee schedule; complex: based on use group, construction type, area, equipment
NJ.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction (municipal construction official) under state oversight
NJ.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations/additions above $5,000 or involving structural/MEP require permit
NJ.stateBuildingOffice: New Jersey Department of Community Affairs (NJDCA) - Division of Codes and Standards
NJ.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - New Jersey Uniform Construction Code (UCC) mandatory statewide

NM.citation: NMSA § 60-13-1 et seq. (Construction Industries Licensing Act)
NM.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; temporary construction trailers; minor repairs
NM.notes: New Mexico CID has statewide authority for construction permits and inspections. In areas without local enforcement, CID is the default permitting authority.
NM.permitFeeStructure: CID: based on construction value; local jurisdictions may have additional fees
NM.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction or State Construction Industries Division (CID)
NM.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations/additions above $5,000 typically require permit
NM.stateBuildingOffice: New Mexico Regulation and Licensing Department - Construction Industries Division (CID)
NM.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - New Mexico Construction Industries Code mandatory statewide; CID administers

adoptedCodes: NY Energy Conservation Construction Code (IECC 2021 with NY amendments)
Uniform Code (IBC 2020 based with NY amendments)
NY.citation: Executive Law § 370 et seq. (Uniform Fire Prevention and Building Code Act)
NY.exemptions: Minor repairs under $20,000 (varies by locality); maintenance items; agricultural buildings on agricultural land
NY.notes: New York City uses its own NYC Building Code (locally enacted but generally consistent with Uniform Code). NYC has particularly complex permit requirements for major construction (Directive 14, etc.).
NY.permitFeeStructure: Varies by jurisdiction; NYC has complex fee schedule; upstate jurisdictions vary widely
NY.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction (city, town, or village building department); NYC has own Department of Buildings
NY.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations/additions above $20,000 or involving structural/MEP require permit in most jurisdictions
NY.stateBuildingOffice: New York State Department of State - Division of Building Standards and Codes
NY.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - New York State Uniform Fire Prevention and Building Code (Uniform Code) mandatory statewide

adoptedCodes: NC Building Code (IBC 2018 with NC amendments)
NC Energy Conservation Code (IECC 2021 with NC amendments)
NC.citation: N.C. Gen. Stat. § 160D-1101 et seq. (NC State Building Code)
NC.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; detached residential accessory structures ≤12 sq ft; temporary structures
NC.notes: NC State Building Code is enforced by county and municipal building inspection departments. State Building Code Council adopts the code. Coastal areas have enhanced hurricane construction requirements.
NC.permitFeeStructure: Varies by county; NC sets fee schedules; typically $X per $1,000 construction value
NC.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction (county or city building inspection department)
NC.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations/additions above $15,000 or involving structural/MEP require permit
NC.stateBuildingOffice: North Carolina Department of Insurance - Engineering Division (Building Code)
NC.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - North Carolina State Building Code mandatory statewide

ND.citation: NDCC § 23-01-37 (State Fire Code); local municipal codes
ND.commercialThreshold: Commercial permits required in incorporated areas
ND.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; many rural unincorporated areas; minor repairs
ND.notes: North Dakota does not have a mandatory statewide building code. Urban areas have adopted codes; rural areas may have minimal requirements.
ND.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction; ND State Plumbing Board and Electrical Board for trades
ND.residentialThreshold: Varies by jurisdiction; many rural areas have no building permit requirement
ND.stateBuildingOffice: North Dakota State Fire Marshal (fire); no statewide building code office
ND.statewideBuildingCode: Partial - No mandatory statewide building code; IFC adopted by State Fire Marshal; specialty trades regulated statewide

OH.commercialThreshold: All commercial/non-residential construction requires state or local permit under OBC
OH.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; detached accessory residential structures under 200 sq ft; minor repairs under $5,000
OH.notes: Ohio has a statewide commercial building code (OBC). Residential code enforced locally. Ohio has detailed plan review and inspection requirements.
OH.permitFeeStructure: Varies by jurisdiction; OBBC fees: based on construction value and use group
OH.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction; OBBC for commercial permits in areas without local department
OH.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit from local building department; alterations/additions above $5,000 or involving structural/MEP require permit
OH.stateBuildingOffice: Ohio Board of Building Standards (BBS) / Ohio Building Professional Association (OBPA)
OH.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Ohio Building Code (OBC) mandatory statewide for commercial/non-residential; local code for residential

OK.commercialThreshold: All commercial construction requires permit in incorporated areas
OK.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; unincorporated areas without local codes; minor repairs
OK.notes: Oklahoma UBCC establishes codes that local governments may adopt. Tornado construction requirements are increasingly common. Some municipalities have adopted enhanced standards.
OK.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction (city or county); no mandatory statewide code for all buildings
OK.residentialThreshold: Permit required in most municipalities; rural areas may have no requirement
OK.statewideBuildingCode: Partial - Oklahoma Uniform Building Code Commission (UBCC) adopts codes; adoption by localities is voluntary for some code types

OR.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; detached accessory structures ≤200 sq ft with no plumbing or electrical; temporary structures
OR.notes: Oregon has a comprehensive statewide building code program. BCD provides oversight; local municipalities are agents for enforcement. Oregon Energy Efficiency Specialty Code (OEESC) applies.
OR.permitFeeStructure: State sets fee schedule components; local jurisdictions may add surcharges; typical $X per $1,000 valuation
OR.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction under BCD oversight; Oregon Building Codes Division (BCD) for state oversight
OR.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations/additions above $1,000 or involving structural/MEP require permit
OR.stateBuildingOffice: Oregon Department of Consumer and Business Services (DCBS) - Building Codes Division (BCD)
OR.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Oregon Structural Specialty Code (OSSC) mandatory statewide

PA.citation: 34 Pa.C.S. § 7101 et seq. (Uniform Construction Code Act)
PA.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; certain detached residential accessory structures; mining operations; temporary construction trailers
PA.notes: Pennsylvania enacted the UCC in 1999 to create statewide code. Municipalities are responsible for enforcement. DLI is the default permitting authority in municipalities that have not opted in.
PA.permitFeeStructure: State-established fee schedule; $X per $1,000 valuation; counties may charge additional
PA.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction; DLI for commercial permits in municipalities without local inspection
PA.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations/additions above $5,000 or involving structural/MEP require permit
PA.stateBuildingOffice: Pennsylvania Department of Labor and Industry (DLI) - Building Code Division
PA.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Pennsylvania Construction Code Act (Uniform Construction Code / UCC) mandatory statewide

adoptedCodes: RI Building Code (IBC 2018 with RI amendments)
RI One and Two Family Dwelling Code (IRC 2018)
RI.citation: R.I. Gen. Laws § 23-27.3-1 et seq. (State Building Code)
RI.exemptions: Minor repairs under $5,000; agricultural buildings; maintenance items
RI.permitFeeStructure: Set by each municipality; state recommends fee schedule
RI.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction (city or town building official) under state oversight
RI.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations/additions above $5,000 or involving structural/MEP require permit
RI.stateBuildingOffice: Rhode Island Department of Business Regulation (DBR) - Building Code Standards Committee (BCSC)
RI.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Rhode Island State Building Code mandatory statewide

adoptedCodes: SC Building Code (IBC 2018 with SC amendments)
SC.citation: S.C. Code Ann. § 6-9-10 et seq. (South Carolina Building Codes)
SC.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; detached accessory structures under 200 sq ft; minor repairs
SC.notes: South Carolina has a statewide building code. Coastal areas (coastal zone) have additional flood and wind construction requirements. Berkeley, Charleston, Dorchester counties in particular have strong enforcement.
SC.permitFeeStructure: Varies by county; typically based on construction value
SC.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction; SCLLR for fire code and certain specialty permits
SC.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations/additions above $5,000 or involving structural/MEP require permit
SC.stateBuildingOffice: South Carolina Department of Labor, Licensing and Regulation (LLR) - Building Codes Division
SC.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - South Carolina Building Code (based on IBC) mandatory statewide

SD.citation: SDCL § 34-29B (State Fire Prevention Code); local codes
SD.commercialThreshold: Commercial permits required in incorporated areas
SD.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; many rural unincorporated areas; minor repairs
SD.notes: South Dakota does not have a mandatory statewide building code for local governments. Code adoption varies by locality.
SD.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction; State Fire Marshal for fire code
SD.residentialThreshold: Varies by jurisdiction; many rural areas have no building permit requirement
SD.statewideBuildingCode: Partial - State Fire Prevention Code mandatory; no mandatory statewide building code for all buildings

TN.citation: TCA § 68-120-101 et seq. (Tennessee Building and Fire Prevention Code)
TN.commercialThreshold: All commercial construction requires state permit through local enforcement
TN.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; detached accessory structures under 144 sq ft
TN.notes: Tennessee adopted statewide building codes with the Tennessee Building and Fire Prevention Code. Both residential and commercial codes are mandatory statewide with local enforcement.
TN.permitFeeStructure: Varies by county; TSBCE: $X per sq ft for commercial review
TN.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction; TSBCE for commercial; State Fire Marshal for fire code
TN.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations above $5,000 or structural/MEP work requires permit
TN.stateBuildingOffice: Tennessee State Building Commission (TSBC) and Department of Commerce and Insurance (DCI) - Fire Prevention Division
TN.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Tennessee Building Codes mandatory statewide for commercial; residential through DARC

adoptedCodes: IBC 2015 (Houston, Dallas, Austin; each may amend locally)
TX.citation: Tex. Gov't Code § 469 (accessibility); local municipal codes
TX.commercialThreshold: All commercial construction requires permit in incorporated areas; TDLR requires registration of commercial construction for accessibility review
TX.exemptions: Unincorporated county areas (counties have no permit authority in Texas except for some platted subdivisions); agricultural buildings; certain temporary structures
TX.notes: Texas counties cannot require building permits by state law. Texas municipalities regulate building through local codes. TDLR requires registration and inspection of all commercial construction projects for accessibility compliance (Texas Accessibility Standards). Significant variation among cities.
TX.permitFeeStructure: Varies significantly by city; no state fee structure
TX.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction (city); no state-mandated building permit for unincorporated areas
TX.residentialThreshold: Varies by city; all major cities require permits for new construction; no state requirement for unincorporated areas
TX.stateBuildingOffice: Texas Department of Licensing and Regulation (TDLR) - Architectural Barriers
TX.statewideBuildingCode: Partial - State Fire Marshal for fire code; TDLR for accessibility (TAS); no mandatory statewide building code

UT.citation: Utah Code § 15A-1-101 et seq. (Utah Construction and Fire Code Act)
UT.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; detached accessory structures under 200 sq ft; minor repairs under $1,000
UT.notes: Utah adopted the Utah Construction and Fire Codes Act to standardize codes statewide. Updates follow model code cycles. Seismic requirements apply along Wasatch Front.
UT.permitFeeStructure: Varies by jurisdiction; state sets minimum standards
UT.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction; DOPL for contractor licensing; SEOC for state buildings
UT.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations/additions above $1,000 or involving structural/MEP require permit
UT.stateBuildingOffice: Utah Division of Facilities and Construction Management (DFCM) for state buildings; local jurisdictions for private
UT.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Utah Building Code mandatory statewide

adoptedCodes: VT Fire and Building Safety Code (IFC based)
VT.citation: 20 V.S.A. § 2901 et seq. (Fire Safety); 10 V.S.A. § 6001 et seq. (Act 250)
VT.commercialThreshold: All commercial construction requires permit; Act 250 applies to commercial projects above threshold
VT.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; minor maintenance; small additions under certain thresholds
VT.notes: Vermont Act 250 is a major land use permit that applies to significant development projects statewide. Vermont RBES requires blower door testing and energy verification for residential construction.
VT.permitFeeStructure: Set by each municipality; Act 250 application fees additional
VT.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction for most projects; Act 250 for larger development; BISHCA for some project types
VT.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations/additions above $1,000 or involving structural/MEP require permit; Act 250 for developments >1 acre or >10 units
VT.stateBuildingOffice: Vermont Department of Public Safety - Division of Fire Safety
VT.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Vermont Residential Building Energy Standards (RBES) mandatory; commercial energy code mandatory; fire code statewide

VA.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; detached accessory structures under 256 sq ft in some localities; minor repairs under $1,000
VA.notes: Virginia USBC is one of the strongest statewide code programs. DHCD is the Board of Housing and Community Development's administrative agency. The Code requires re-inspection fees and detailed documentation.
VA.permitFeeStructure: Varies by locality; DHCD sets minimum fee schedule
VA.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction (county or city building official) under DHCD oversight
VA.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations/additions above $5,000 or involving structural/MEP require permit
VA.stateBuildingOffice: Virginia Department of Housing and Community Development (DHCD) - Building and Fire Regulation
VA.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Virginia Uniform Statewide Building Code (USBC) mandatory statewide

adoptedCodes: Washington State Energy Code (WSEC 2021 - IECC based with significant WA amendments)
WA.citation: RCW § 19.27 (State Building Code Act); RCW § 19.27A (Washington State Energy Code)
WA.exemptions: Agricultural structures; detached accessory structures ≤200 sq ft (some jurisdictions); minor repairs
WA.notes: Washington has one of the most stringent energy codes in the nation (WSEC). Seismic requirements significant throughout state. SBCC adopts and updates state building codes on regular cycle.
WA.permitFeeStructure: Varies by jurisdiction; WSBC sets minimum standards; major cities charge premium fees
WA.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction (city or county) under SBCC oversight
WA.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations/additions above $2,000 or involving structural/MEP require permit
WA.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Washington State Building Code (WSBC) mandatory statewide

WV.citation: W. Va. Code § 29-3-1 et seq. (State Fire Code); § 21-11-1 (building contractors)
WV.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; single-family homes in some rural unincorporated areas; minor repairs
WV.notes: West Virginia has a patchwork of building code adoption. Some rural areas have limited building permit requirements.
WV.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction; State Fire Marshal for fire code; WVDO for commercial in some areas
WV.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit in municipalities; rural areas vary
WV.stateBuildingOffice: West Virginia Division of Labor (WVDO) - Building Code
WV.statewideBuildingCode: Partial - State Fire Code mandatory; WV State Building Code for commercial/multi-family; local residential codes vary

adoptedCodes: Wis. Commercial Building Code (IBC 2015 with WI amendments)
WI.citation: Wis. Stat. § 101.01 et seq. (Commercial Building Safety); § 101.60 et seq. (Residential Building Safety)
WI.exemptions: Agricultural buildings (partial); detached residential garages up to 600 sq ft in some areas; minor repairs
WI.notes: Wisconsin has a comprehensive statewide code. DSPS enforces code in municipalities that opt not to have local inspectors. SBD provides plan review for commercial projects.
WI.permitFeeStructure: Varies by municipality; DSPS: based on construction value
WI.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction; DSPS for commercial permits in areas without local inspection
WI.residentialThreshold: All new residential construction requires permit; alterations/additions above $5,000 or involving structural/MEP require permit
WI.stateBuildingOffice: Wisconsin Department of Safety and Professional Services (DSPS) - Safety and Buildings Division
WI.statewideBuildingCode: Yes - Wisconsin Commercial Building Code and Residential Building Code mandatory statewide

WY.citation: Wyo. Stat. § 35-9-101 et seq. (Fire Prevention and Electrical Safety); local municipal codes
WY.commercialThreshold: Commercial permits required in incorporated areas
WY.exemptions: Agricultural buildings; many rural unincorporated areas; minor repairs
WY.notes: Wyoming does not have a mandatory statewide building code for all buildings. Individual municipalities adopt codes. Rural areas may have minimal requirements.
WY.permittingAuthority: Local jurisdiction; State Fire Marshal for fire code
WY.residentialThreshold: Varies by jurisdiction; many rural areas have no building permit requirement
WY.statewideBuildingCode: Partial - State Fire Code mandatory; no mandatory statewide building code for local governments

AL.authority: Alabama Licensing Board for General Contractors

AK.notes: No statewide contractor license; licensing handled by municipalities. Anchorage and Fairbanks require local contractor licenses. Specialty trades (electricians, plumbers) are licensed by the state.

AZ.notes: Bond amounts vary by license class and dollar volume; minimum listed. Residential contractors require separate CR license.

AR.notes: Residential license required for projects >$2,000; commercial license required for projects >$20,000.

CA.notes: $500 threshold for single contract; CSLB bond is $15,000 (contractor's bond). Workers' comp certificate required unless sole owner with no employees. License required for all work $500 or more combined labor and materials.

CO.notes: No statewide general contractor license; licensing handled by local jurisdictions (Denver, Boulder, etc.). State licenses electricians, plumbers, and HVAC contractors through DORA. Always verify local requirements.

CT.notes: HIC registration required for home improvement contracts >$200. New home construction requires separate contractor registration. No statewide commercial GC license beyond local requirements.

DE.authority: Delaware Division of Revenue / Local Jurisdictions
DE.notes: Home improvement contractors must be licensed with the state. Wilmington and New Castle County have additional local requirements for commercial contractors.

DC.authority: District of Columbia Department of Licensing and Consumer Protection (DLCP)
DC.notes: District business licensing applies citywide. Home Improvement Contractor registration and surety bonding are commonly required for residential work; project-specific permits are coordinated through DC permitting portals.

FL.notes: 14 hours of CE required every 2 years for renewal. Certified contractors may work statewide; registered contractors limited to jurisdiction that issued registration. Workers' comp exemptions available for sole proprietors.

GA.authority: Georgia State Licensing Board for Residential and General Contractors
GA.notes: Residential-Basic license for projects up to $500,000; Residential-Light Commercial for projects up to $1,000,000. Net worth requirements apply for higher classes.

HI.authority: Hawaii Department of Commerce and Consumer Affairs (DCCA) - Professional and Vocational Licensing
HI.notes: All work over $1,000 requires a license. Bond amounts vary by annual volume. Separate contractor license required for each island county.

ID.authority: Idaho Department of Labor (Public Works) / Division of Building Safety (trades)
ID.notes: No statewide GC license for private construction. Public works contractors must register with the Department of Labor for projects over $10,000. Electricians, plumbers, and HVAC contractors are licensed by the Division of Building Safety.

IL.authority: Illinois Department of Financial and Professional Regulation (IDFPR)
IL.notes: No statewide general contractor license for commercial or residential GC work. Illinois licenses roofing contractors statewide. Chicago, Cook County, and many municipalities have local contractor licensing requirements.

IN.authority: Indiana Professional Licensing Agency (PLA) / Office of the Indiana Attorney General (HIC)
IN.notes: Home improvement contractors must register with the AG's office. No statewide commercial GC license. Plumbers and electricians licensed separately. Indianapolis and other municipalities may have additional local requirements.

IA.authority: Local Jurisdictions / Iowa Utilities Board (specialty trades)
IA.notes: No statewide general contractor license. Electricians and plumbers licensed by the state. Many municipalities including Des Moines and Cedar Rapids have local contractor licensing requirements.

KS.authority: Local Jurisdictions / Kansas State Board of Technical Professions (specialty)
KS.notes: No statewide general contractor license. Wichita, Overland Park, and other municipalities have local contractor licensing requirements. Specialty trades (electrical, plumbing) licensed separately.

KY.authority: Kentucky Department of Housing, Buildings and Construction
KY.notes: Contractors performing construction work over $10,000 must be licensed. Bond and insurance amounts vary by class.

LA.authority: Louisiana State Licensing Board for Contractors
LA.notes: Residential contractors separate from commercial. Board administers its own trade-specific exams. NASCLA Accredited exam accepted for commercial reciprocity.

ME.authority: Maine Department of Professional and Financial Regulation - Contractors' Registration Program
ME.notes: Residential contractors and home improvement contractors must register with the state. Commercial contractors not licensed at the state level beyond local requirements.

MD.authority: Maryland Home Improvement Commission (MHIC) / Maryland Home Builder Registration Unit
MD.notes: All home improvement work over $100 requires MHIC license. Home builders require separate MHBR registration. Commercial contractors governed by local jurisdictions.

MA.authority: Massachusetts Office of Consumer Affairs and Business Regulation
MA.notes: CSL required for structural work on 1-2 family residences. HIC registration required for home improvement projects. 12 CE hours required per 2-year renewal cycle for CSL.

MI.authority: Michigan Department of Licensing and Regulatory Affairs (LARA) - Bureau of Construction Codes
MI.notes: Residential builder license required for construction/alteration of 1-2 family homes. No statewide commercial GC license; commercial governed by local jurisdictions. 3 CE hours per 3-year renewal.

MN.authority: Minnesota Department of Labor and Industry - Construction Codes and Licensing
MN.notes: Residential contractor license required for new construction or remodeling over $15,000. No statewide commercial GC license. 14 CE hours per 2-year renewal. Minneapolis-St. Paul metropolitan area has additional requirements.

MS.notes: License required for commercial projects over $50,000 and residential projects over $10,000. Separate residential contractor license.

MO.authority: Local Jurisdictions / Missouri Division of Professional Registration (specialty)
licenseClasses: Electrical, Plumbing (specialty only at state level)
MO.notes: No statewide general contractor license. Kansas City, St. Louis, and most municipalities have local contractor licensing requirements. Electricians and plumbers licensed by state Division of Professional Registration.

MT.authority: Montana Department of Labor and Industry - Building Codes Bureau
MT.notes: All contractors must register with the state. No comprehensive GC license exam; registration-based system. Electricians, plumbers, and HVAC contractors hold separate licenses.

NE.authority: Local Jurisdictions / Nebraska Electrical Division and Plumbing Division (specialty)
NE.notes: No statewide general contractor license. Omaha, Lincoln, and most municipalities have local contractor licensing. Electricians licensed by the Nebraska Electrical Division; plumbers licensed by the State Plumbing Board.

NV.notes: Bond amounts vary significantly by license classification and annual volume. Residential work requires workers' comp or affidavit for sole operators. Active license list public on NSCB website.

NH.authority: NH Office of Professional Licensure and Certification / NH Insurance Dept (HIC bond)
NH.notes: No comprehensive statewide GC license. Home improvement contractors must register. Electricians and plumbers licensed by the OPLC. Local municipalities may have additional requirements.

NJ.authority: New Jersey Division of Consumer Affairs - Contractor's Registration Act
NJ.notes: Home improvement contractors must register with NJ DCA. $500,000 liability insurance required; bond alternatives acceptable. New home builders registered with DCA separately. No statewide commercial GC license.

NM.notes: License required for construction work over $10,000. CID also handles building permit and inspection services. RB-98 license for residential; GB-98 for commercial. Separate specialty trade licenses.

NY.authority: Local Jurisdictions (NYC DCA, Westchester, Nassau, Suffolk counties)
NY.notes: No statewide general contractor license. New York City requires a Home Improvement Contractor license from the DCA. Nassau, Suffolk, and Westchester counties and NYC boroughs have local licensing. Electricians and plumbers licensed locally.

NC.authority: North Carolina Licensing Board for General Contractors
NC.notes: License required for projects over $30,000. Net worth requirements replace bond for higher license classes. Unlimited license requires minimum $17,000 net worth; no cap.

ND.notes: Contractor registration required for projects over $2,000. Registration-based system, not exam-based. Specialty trades (electricians, plumbers) licensed by separate state boards.

OH.authority: Ohio Construction Industry Licensing Board (OCILB) / Local jurisdictions for GC
OH.notes: No statewide general contractor license; general building contractors governed by local jurisdictions. OCILB licenses electrical, plumbing, HVAC, hydronics, and refrigeration contractors statewide. 24 CE hours per 3-year renewal for trade licenses.

OK.notes: License required for projects over $2,500. Separate residential specialty contractor license. Oklahoma City and Tulsa may have additional local requirements.

OR.notes: All construction work requires CCB license. 16 CE hours required per renewal for residential contractors. Lead Renovation certification required for pre-1978 homes. Bond amounts depend on contractor type and annual revenue.

PA.authority: Pennsylvania Office of Attorney General - Bureau of Consumer Protection
PA.notes: No statewide general contractor license exam required. Home Improvement Contractors must register under HICPA for residential work >$500. $50,000 financial protection required (surety bond or bank account escrow). Philadelphia and Pittsburgh have local contractor licensing requirements.

RI.authority: Rhode Island Department of Business Regulation - Contractors' Registration and Licensing Board
RI.notes: All contractors performing work in excess of $1,000 must be registered or licensed. Residential contractors require Class C license. Reciprocity agreements with CT and MA for some license categories.

SC.notes: 8 CE hours required every 2 years. License required for projects over $5,000 in value. Residential specialty contractors have separate licensing.

SD.authority: Local Jurisdictions / South Dakota State Electrical Commission (specialty)
SD.notes: No statewide general contractor license. Sioux Falls, Rapid City, and many municipalities have local licensing requirements. Electricians and plumbers licensed by state.

TN.notes: License required for commercial projects >$25,000 and residential projects >$3,000. Separate electrical, plumbing, and HVAC licensing. NASCLA commercial exam accepted.

TX.authority: Texas Department of Licensing and Regulation (TDLR) - specific trades only
TX.notes: No statewide general contractor license. TDLR licenses HVAC, plumbing, electrical, and other specialty trades. Workers' comp not required by state law but recommended. Dallas, Houston, San Antonio, Austin, and most Texas cities have local contractor registration or licensing requirements.

UT.authority: Utah Division of Occupational and Professional Licensing (DOPL)
UT.notes: License required for construction exceeding $3,000. Bond amounts vary by classification. No CE requirements for general contractors but specialty trades may differ.

VT.authority: Vermont Office of Professional Regulation (specialty trades only)
VT.notes: No statewide general contractor license. Electricians, plumbers, and fire suppression contractors are licensed by the Office of Professional Regulation. Local jurisdictions may have contractor requirements.

VA.authority: Virginia Department of Professional and Occupational Regulation (DPOR)
VA.notes: License required for any construction work totaling $1,000 or more. Class determined by annual revenue. Trades (electricians, plumbers) licensed separately by DPOR. Home Improvement Salesperson license also required.

WA.authority: Washington State Department of Labor and Industries (L&I)
WA.notes: All contractors must register with L&I. $12,000 bond and $200,000 liability insurance required for general contractors ($6,000 bond and $200,000 insurance for specialty). Electrical contractors licensed separately by L&I Electrical Program.

WV.notes: License required for all contracting work over $2,500. Bond amounts vary by license class. Home improvement contractors require separate registration.

WI.authority: Wisconsin Department of Safety and Professional Services (DSPS)
WI.notes: Dwelling Contractor Qualifier required for all new one- and two-family home construction. 12 CE hours per renewal for residential. No statewide commercial GC license; commercial governed by local jurisdictions. Milwaukee and Madison have local requirements.

WY.authority: Local Jurisdictions / Wyoming Department of Fire Prevention and Electrical Safety (specialty)
licenseClasses: Electrical, Plumbing (specialty only at state level)
WY.notes: No statewide general contractor license. Cheyenne, Casper, and many municipalities have local contractor licensing requirements. Electricians licensed by the Department of Fire Prevention and Electrical Safety.

AL.antiIndemnityNote: Ala. Code § 8-1-42 prohibits hold harmless clauses in construction contracts that indemnify for indemnitee's own negligence
AL.impliedWarrantyNote: Alabama recognizes implied warranty of habitability for residential construction; Cochran v. Keeton, 252 So.2d 289 (Ala. 1971)

AK.antiIndemnityNote: AS § 45.45.900 prohibits indemnification provisions that require a party to indemnify for the other party's own negligence
AK.impliedWarrantyNote: Alaska recognizes implied warranty of workmanship for residential construction

AZ.antiIndemnityNote: A.R.S. § 32-1159 prohibits provisions in construction contracts that require a party to indemnify another party from liability for that other party's own negligence
AZ.impliedWarrantyNote: Arizona recognizes implied warranty of habitability and workmanship for new residential construction; Centricut, LLC v. J.D. Bassett, etc.
AZ.notes: Arizona Purchaser Dwelling Act (Right to Repair) requires pre-litigation notice and opportunity to cure for construction defects in new residential construction.
AZ.rightToRepairCitation: A.R.S. § 12-1361 et seq. (Arizona Purchaser Dwelling Act)

AR.antiIndemnityNote: Ark. Code Ann. § 22-9-214 limits indemnification provisions in construction contracts on public works; § 4-56-104 for private
AR.impliedWarrantyNote: Arkansas recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new home construction; Smith v. Old Warson Dev. Co.

CA.antiIndemnityNote: Cal. Civ. Code § 2782 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts for the indemnitee's own active negligence; § 2782.05 for residential
CA.citation: Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 337 et seq.; Cal. Civ. Code § 895; § 2782
CA.impliedWarrantyNote: California has detailed Right to Repair Act (SB 800) functionality standards for new residential construction; replaces traditional implied warranty for builder-sold homes in most cases
CA.notes: California Right to Repair Act (SB 800) provides specific functionality standards for newly constructed residences. Pay-if-paid clauses are not enforceable in California; pay-when-paid clauses create a reasonable time period. Lien waivers must follow specific statutory forms.
CA.rightToRepairCitation: Cal. Civ. Code § 895 et seq. (Right to Repair Act / SB 800)

CO.antiIndemnityNote: C.R.S. § 13-21-111.5 comparative fault limits indemnification; case law limits broad indemnification provisions
CO.impliedWarrantyNote: Colorado recognizes implied warranty of suitability for new home construction; Barnes v. Mac Brown & Co.
CO.notes: Colorado Construction Defect Action Reform Act (CDARA) requires a pre-litigation notice and opportunity to cure process for construction defect claims. HOA construction defect lawsuits require unit owner vote.
CO.rightToRepairCitation: C.R.S. § 13-20-803.5 (Construction Defect Reform Act - mandatory pre-litigation process)

CT.antiIndemnityNote: CGS § 52-572k limits indemnity clauses in construction contracts that would indemnify for the indemnitee's own negligence
CT.impliedWarrantyNote: Connecticut recognizes implied warranty of workmanship and habitability for new residential construction
CT.notes: Connecticut New Home Construction Warranties Act provides warranties for new homes: 1 year for workmanship, 2 years for MEP systems, 3-year for structural components.

DE.antiIndemnityNote: Delaware does not have a specific anti-indemnity statute; broad indemnification provisions may be enforceable
DE.impliedWarrantyNote: Delaware recognizes implied warranty of fitness and habitability for new residential construction

FL.antiIndemnityNote: Fla. Stat. § 725.06 limits indemnification provisions in construction contracts; insurance requirements for indemnification
FL.impliedWarrantyNote: Florida recognizes implied warranty of fitness and habitability; Florida Statute Chapter 553 sets construction standards
FL.notes: Florida Chapter 558 requires pre-litigation notice and opportunity to cure for construction defect claims. Statutory lien waivers must follow prescribed forms. Contractor must provide disclosure of lien rights to residential owners.
FL.rightToRepairCitation: Fla. Stat. § 558.001 et seq. (Construction Defects Chapter 558)

GA.antiIndemnityNote: O.C.G.A. § 13-8-2(b) limits certain indemnification provisions in construction contracts that shift liability for one's own negligence
GA.impliedWarrantyNote: Georgia recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction; Regency Residential v. Benefiel

HI.antiIndemnityNote: HRS § 431:10-222 restricts indemnification provisions that would require a party to indemnify for another's sole negligence or willful misconduct
HI.impliedWarrantyNote: Hawaii recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction

ID.antiIndemnityNote: Idaho Code § 29-114 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that indemnify for the indemnitee's own negligence or fault
ID.impliedWarrantyNote: Idaho recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction; Bethlahmy v. Bechtel

IL.antiIndemnityNote: 740 ILCS 35/1 (Construction Contract Indemnification for Negligence Act) prohibits indemnification provisions that require a party to indemnify for another's own negligence
IL.impliedWarrantyNote: Illinois recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction; Petersen v. Hubschman Construction Co.
IL.notes: Illinois has a 10-year statute of limitations for written contracts and a 10-year statute of repose for construction defects. Illinois Mechanics Lien Act requires lien waiver compliance.

IN.antiIndemnityNote: IC 26-2-5-1 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that would indemnify a party for its own negligence or willful misconduct
IN.impliedWarrantyNote: Indiana recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction; Theis v. Heuer

IA.antiIndemnityNote: Iowa Code § 573.34 limits broad indemnification in construction contracts on public projects; case law limits private
IA.impliedWarrantyNote: Iowa recognizes implied warranty of workmanship for new residential construction
IA.notes: Iowa has a 15-year statute of repose for construction defects, one of the longest in the nation.

KS.antiIndemnityNote: K.S.A. 16-121 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that indemnify for the indemnitee's own negligence
KS.impliedWarrantyNote: Kansas recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction

KY.antiIndemnityNote: KRS 371.400 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that would indemnify a party for its own negligence
KY.impliedWarrantyNote: Kentucky recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction; Crawley v. Terhune

LA.antiIndemnityNote: La. Rev. Stat. § 9:2780 (OILFIELD ANTI-INDEMNITY) prohibits indemnification in oilfield contracts; § 9:2780.1 for construction contracts
LA.citation: La. Civ. Code art. 2762; La. Rev. Stat. § 9:2772; § 9:2780.1
LA.impliedWarrantyNote: Louisiana Civil Code Article 2762 provides warranty against redhibitory defects; contractors warrant construction for 10 years under redhibition
LA.notes: Louisiana uses civil law (French/Spanish origins) which differs significantly from common law states. The warranty against redhibitory defects (La. Civ. Code arts. 2520-2548) and Contractor's 10-Year Warranty (art. 2762) are unique to Louisiana civil law.

ME.antiIndemnityNote: 14 M.R.S.A. § 8113 prohibits indemnification in construction contracts that would indemnify a party for its own negligence
ME.impliedWarrantyNote: Maine recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction

MD.antiIndemnityNote: Md. Code Ann., Real Prop. § 14-501 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that indemnify for one's own negligence; limited to public and private construction contracts
MD.citation: Md. Code Ann., Cts. & Jud. Proc. § 5-101; § 5-108; Real Prop. § 14-501
MD.impliedWarrantyNote: Maryland recognizes implied warranty of fitness and habitability for new residential construction; Brewer v. Iota Engineering
MD.notes: Maryland has a very long 20-year statute of repose for construction defects — among the longest in the nation. Maryland lien law requires specific lien waiver forms.
MD.statSOLCitation: Md. Code Ann., Cts. & Jud. Proc. § 5-101
MD.statSORCitation: Md. Code Ann., Cts. & Jud. Proc. § 5-108

MA.antiIndemnityNote: M.G.L. c. 149, § 29C prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that indemnify for the indemnitee's own negligence
MA.impliedWarrantyNote: Massachusetts recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction; Albrecht v. Clifford
MA.notes: Massachusetts does not enforce pay-if-paid clauses (pay-when-paid only creates a reasonable time for payment). Massachusetts anti-indemnity statute is strictly enforced.

MI.antiIndemnityNote: MCL § 691.991 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that would indemnify a party for its own negligence or willful misconduct
MI.impliedWarrantyNote: Michigan recognizes implied warranty of fitness for new residential construction; Weeks v. Slavick Builders, Inc.

MN.antiIndemnityNote: Minn. Stat. § 337.02 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that would require a party to indemnify for the other party's own negligence
MN.impliedWarrantyNote: Minnesota recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction; Keene v. Brigham
MN.notes: Minnesota New Home Warranty Act provides mandatory implied warranties for new home construction. Anti-indemnity statute is broadly applied.
MN.rightToRepairCitation: Minn. Stat. § 327A (New Home Warranty Act) - 1-year workmanship, 2-year systems, 10-year structural

MS.antiIndemnityNote: Mississippi does not have a specific construction anti-indemnity statute; general enforceability depends on case law
MS.impliedWarrantyNote: Mississippi recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction; Keyes v. Guy Bailey Homes, Inc.
MS.rightToRepairCitation: Miss. Code Ann. § 83-58-1 et seq. (Residential Property Warranty Act)

MO.antiIndemnityNote: Mo. Rev. Stat. § 434.100 limits indemnification provisions in construction contracts to prohibit indemnification for the indemnitee's own negligence
MO.impliedWarrantyNote: Missouri recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction; Smith v. Old Warson Dev. Co.

MT.antiIndemnityNote: Mont. Code Ann. § 28-2-2111 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that would require a party to indemnify for the other party's negligence
MT.impliedWarrantyNote: Montana recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction

NE.antiIndemnityNote: Neb. Rev. Stat. § 25-21,187 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that would require a party to indemnify for the other party's negligence
NE.impliedWarrantyNote: Nebraska recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction; Karbas v. Fidelity National Title Ins. Co.

NV.antiIndemnityNote: NRS § 108.2463 limits indemnification provisions in construction contracts to prohibit indemnification for sole negligence
NV.impliedWarrantyNote: Nevada recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction; Newmar Corporation v. Sherrill
NV.notes: Nevada Chapter 40 requires pre-litigation notice and opportunity to cure for construction defect claims in new residential construction. Very detailed process with specific timelines.
NV.rightToRepairCitation: NRS § 40.600 et seq. (Chapter 40 - Constructional Defect Law)

NH.antiIndemnityNote: RSA 338-A:1 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that would indemnify a party for its own negligence
NH.impliedWarrantyNote: New Hampshire recognizes implied warranty of workmanship for new residential construction; Lempke v. Dagenais

NJ.antiIndemnityNote: N.J.S.A. 2A:40A-1 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that require a party to indemnify for another's negligence
NJ.impliedWarrantyNote: New Jersey recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction; Schipper v. Levitt & Sons, Inc.
NJ.notes: New Jersey New Home Warranty Act provides mandatory warranties for new homes: 1-year defects, 2-year MEP systems, 10-year structural defects. Builder registration required.
NJ.rightToRepairCitation: N.J.S.A. 46:3B-1 et seq. (New Home Warranty and Builders' Registration Act)

NM.antiIndemnityNote: NMSA § 56-7-1 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that would indemnify for the indemnitee's own negligence
NM.impliedWarrantyNote: New Mexico recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction; Lohse v. Atlantic Richfield Co.
NM.notes: New Mexico Construction Defect Claims Act requires pre-litigation notice and opportunity to cure for construction defect claims. Anti-indemnity statute strictly applied.
NM.rightToRepairCitation: NMSA § 42-12-1 et seq. (Construction Defect Claims Act)

NY.antiIndemnityNote: General Obligations Law § 5-322.1 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that would indemnify for the indemnitee's own negligence; exception for additional insured status
NY.impliedWarrantyNote: New York recognizes implied warranty of habitability under Multiple Dwelling Law and Real Property Law for residential construction
NY.notes: New York has a short 3-year discovery rule for construction defect claims (statute of repose is limited). NYC construction contracts must comply with NYC Administrative Code requirements. Lien law requires payment within specific timeframes.

NC.antiIndemnityNote: N.C. Gen. Stat. § 22B-1 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that would require a party to indemnify for the other party's negligence
NC.impliedWarrantyNote: North Carolina recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction; Hartley v. Ballou
NC.notes: North Carolina does not enforce pay-if-paid clauses. Anti-indemnity statute is strictly enforced. NC has a 3-year statute of limitations and 6-year statute of repose for construction defects.

ND.antiIndemnityNote: N.D. Cent. Code § 9-08-02.1 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that would indemnify for the indemnitee's own negligence
ND.impliedWarrantyNote: North Dakota recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction

OH.antiIndemnityNote: ORC § 4113.62(B) limits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that would indemnify a party for its own negligence or willful misconduct
OH.impliedWarrantyNote: Ohio recognizes implied warranty of good workmanship and habitability for new residential construction; Jones v. Centex Homes
OH.notes: Ohio has a prompt payment act (ORC § 4113.61) that applies to private commercial and residential construction.

OK.antiIndemnityNote: 15 O.S. § 221 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that would indemnify a party for its own negligence
OK.impliedWarrantyNote: Oklahoma recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction; Kirk v. Ridgway
OK.rightToRepairCitation: 15 O.S. § 841 et seq. (Dwelling Unit Product Liability Act)

OR.antiIndemnityNote: ORS § 30.140 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that would indemnify for the indemnitee's own negligence
OR.impliedWarrantyNote: Oregon recognizes implied warranty of good workmanship for new residential construction; Yepsen v. Burgess
OR.notes: Oregon Construction Contractors Board (CCB) dispute resolution process for residential construction defects. Contractors must be licensed with CCB.
OR.rightToRepairCitation: ORS § 701.565 et seq. (Residential Construction Dispute Resolution)

PA.antiIndemnityNote: 73 P.S. § 511 (Fair Contracting for Services Act) limits indemnification in certain construction contracts; case law also limits broad indemnification
PA.impliedWarrantyNote: Pennsylvania recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction; Elderkin v. Gaster
PA.notes: Pennsylvania has a 12-year statute of repose for construction defects (among the longer periods nationally). CASPA (Contractor and Subcontractor Payment Act) governs payment disputes in construction.

RI.antiIndemnityNote: R.I. Gen. Laws § 6-34-1 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that would require a party to indemnify for the other party's own negligence
RI.impliedWarrantyNote: Rhode Island recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction

SC.antiIndemnityNote: S.C. Code Ann. § 32-2-10 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that would indemnify for the indemnitee's sole negligence or intentional acts
SC.impliedWarrantyNote: South Carolina recognizes implied warranty of workmanship for new residential construction; Lane v. Trenholm Building Co.
SC.rightToRepairCitation: S.C. Code Ann. § 40-59-810 et seq. (Residential Property Condition Disclosure Act and similar)

SD.antiIndemnityNote: South Dakota does not have a specific construction anti-indemnity statute; general enforceability depends on case law and public policy
SD.impliedWarrantyNote: South Dakota recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction

TN.antiIndemnityNote: TCA § 62-6-123 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that would indemnify a party for its own negligence
TN.impliedWarrantyNote: Tennessee recognizes implied warranty of habitability and workmanship for new residential construction; Dixon v. Mountain City Construction Co.
TN.notes: Tennessee has a short 4-year statute of repose for construction defects. Tennessee Right to Cure Act requires pre-litigation notice and opportunity to cure.
TN.rightToRepairCitation: TCA § 66-36-101 et seq. (Tennessee Right to Cure Construction Defects)

TX.antiIndemnityNote: Tex. Ins. Code § 151.102 (COIA) and Tex. Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code § 130.002 prohibit certain indemnification and additional insured provisions in construction contracts
TX.citation: Tex. Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code § 16.004; § 16.009; Tex. Prop. Code § 27.001
TX.impliedWarrantyNote: Texas recognizes implied warranty of good workmanship and habitability for residential construction; Melody Home Manufacturing Co. v. Barnes
TX.notes: Texas RCLA requires 60-day notice and opportunity to inspect/repair before filing suit for residential construction defects. Texas Construction of Indemnity Act (COIA) strictly limits indemnification in construction contracts. Statutory lien waivers required.
TX.rightToRepairCitation: Tex. Prop. Code § 27.001 et seq. (Residential Construction Liability Act / RCLA)

UT.antiIndemnityNote: Utah Code § 13-8-1 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that would require a party to indemnify for the other party's negligence
UT.impliedWarrantyNote: Utah recognizes implied warranty of workmanship for new residential construction; Petersen v. Riverton City
UT.notes: Utah has a Residence Lien Restriction and Lien Recovery Fund Act that limits lien exposure on residential construction and provides a state recovery fund for homeowners.
UT.rightToRepairCitation: Utah Code § 78B-4-501 et seq. (Residence Lien Restriction and Lien Recovery Fund Act) and § 78B-6-1501 et seq. (Right to Repair Act)

VT.antiIndemnityNote: 9 V.S.A. § 4601 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that require a party to indemnify for the other party's own negligence
VT.impliedWarrantyNote: Vermont recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction; Auclair v. Bolduc

VA.antiIndemnityNote: Code of Va. § 11-4.1 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that would indemnify for the indemnitee's own negligence
VA.impliedWarrantyNote: Virginia recognizes implied warranty of habitability for new residential construction; Speight v. Moore
VA.notes: Virginia has a short 5-year statute of repose for construction defects. Virginia Prompt Payment Act requires timely payment in construction contracts.
VA.rightToRepairCitation: Code of Va. § 55.1-2900 et seq. (Virginia Property Owners' Association Act) and construction defect pre-litigation procedures

WA.antiIndemnityNote: RCW § 4.24.115 prohibits indemnification provisions in construction contracts that would indemnify for the indemnitee's own negligence
WA.impliedWarrantyNote: Washington recognizes implied warranty of habitability and workmanship for new residential construction; Alejandre v. Bull
WA.notes: Washington Construction Defect Claims Act requires pre-litigation notice and opportunity to cure. Lien rights must be preserved with timely preliminary notice. Pay-if-paid clauses are generally enforceable if clear and conspicuous.
